text.title: Cash Flow Projection and Runway Calculation
text.summary: 3-month and 12-month cash flow tables, runway formula, safety targets by stage, and agent injection requirements.

rule.statement: Build the 12-month forecast bottom-up from known customer count and ARPU — not top-down from desired revenue.
rule.rationale: Top-down forecasts start from a wish number and work backward. Bottom-up forecasts start from real customer data and produce grounded projections that identify actual growth gaps.

rule.statement: Always inject real numbers as context before asking the agent to build projections. Agents will hallucinate specific financial figures if not given actual data.
rule.rationale: Financial hallucinations in projections are silent failures — the output looks plausible but is based on fabricated inputs. Real data must be provided explicitly.

example.note: Agent prompt: "MRR = $8,500, monthly expenses = $4,200, cash balance = $22,000. Build a 3-month cash flow table. Flag if runway drops below 3 months." This gives the agent real numbers to work with.

section.title: Runway Formula

rule.statement: Runway (months) = Cash on hand / Monthly burn rate. Minimum safe runway by stage: pre-revenue = 12+ months, early revenue = 6+ months, growing = 3+ months, profitable = 3+ months (emergency fund).
rule.rationale: Runway is the primary financial safety metric. Below 3 months, a single bad month becomes an existential threat. The emergency fund (3x monthly expenses, separate account) is the profitable-stage equivalent.

section.reference: Pull MRR from Stripe API and calculate runway with warning at 3-month threshold

rule.statement: Run two scenarios each quarter: base case (flat growth) and stress case (20% revenue drop). Stress case sets minimum acceptable runway and spend floor.
rule.rationale: A base-case-only plan has no resilience mechanism. The stress case forces identification of which expenses are cuttable and which are fixed, before the crisis occurs.

rule.statement: Track CAC and LTV monthly alongside P&L. A healthy margin with deteriorating LTV:CAC ratio signals future cash flow risk even when current numbers look good.
rule.rationale: LTV:CAC deterioration predicts future revenue decline before it appears in the P&L. Catching it early allows course correction.
